hero.heading: ⚙️ Kubernetes - Orchestrate Containers at Scale
hero.subtitle: Automatically manage, scale, and deploy containers across multiple machines with zero downtime.
hero.whyLearn: Industry standard used by Google, Netflix, Airbnb. If you run containers in production, you need Kubernetes.

storytelling.problem: You have 100 Docker containers. One crashes—manual restart takes 5 minutes and your app is down. Black Friday: you need 500 containers. How do you update without downtime?
storytelling.solution: Kubernetes automatically manages everything. Restarts crashed containers instantly. Scales to thousands with one command. Rolling updates with zero downtime. Intelligent traffic routing.
storytelling.analogy: Docker is a shipping container. Kubernetes is the entire port with cranes, scheduling, inventory, and self-healing. Container ship breaks? Port auto-reroutes. Demand up? Port builds more ships.
storytelling.realWorldExample: Netflix runs millions of containers using K8s. New feature: deploy to 5% traffic, monitor, continue or rollback—zero user impact.
storytelling.deepDive: Kubernetes is declarative. You describe desired state (want 5 replicas), K8s maintains it. Master nodes decide, worker nodes execute.

architectureFlow.title: Define Application
architectureFlow.description: Write YAML files describing containers, resources, networking
architectureFlow.details: Deployment, Service, ConfigMap describe everything your app needs.

architectureFlow.title: Deploy to Cluster
architectureFlow.description: Submit YAML to Kubernetes API (kubectl apply)
architectureFlow.details: Control plane validates and schedules containers on nodes.

architectureFlow.title: Auto-Healing & Scaling
architectureFlow.description: Kubernetes monitors health, replaces failed pods, scales on demand
architectureFlow.details: Crash? New pod starts. CPU high? Scales up. Network issues? Auto-restarts.

architectureFlow.title: Service Discovery
architectureFlow.description: Containers find each other using DNS, load balancing automatic
architectureFlow.details: Apps use service names, not IPs. Services handle load balancing.

beginner.description: Smallest unit. Wraps 1+ containers sharing network/storage.
Exposes pods internally/externally with DNS and load balancing.

intermediate.description: Route external HTTP(S) traffic based on hostnames/paths.
Manage stateful apps with stable identity.

commands.whatItDoes: Shows API server endpoint and services
commands.realUseCase: Verify cluster is accessible and ready for deployments
commands.example:
kubectl cluster-info
Kubernetes control plane is running at https://192.168.1.100:6443
commands.commonMistake: Running without kubeconfig set gets connection refused errors

commands.whatItDoes: Shows node status, roles, and resource usage
commands.realUseCase: Verify all nodes are healthy before deploying applications
commands.example:
kubectl get nodes
NAME  STATUS  ROLES   VERSION
node1 Ready   worker  v1.27.0
node2 Ready   worker  v1.27.0
commands.commonMistake: Ignoring NotReady status without investigating the problem

commands.whatItDoes: Creates or updates Deployment, Service, ConfigMap from YAML
commands.realUseCase: Primary way to deploy apps to Kubernetes. Used in all CI/CD pipelines.
commands.example:
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
deployment.apps/my-app created
service/my-app created
commands.commonMistake: Forgetting to apply after editing YAML—changes won't take effect!

commands.description: List pods in a specific namespace
commands.whatItDoes: Shows running/pending/failed pods with restart count and age
commands.realUseCase: Check if app pods are running and healthy. Monitor pod status continuously.
commands.example:
kubectl get pods -n production
NAME              READY STATUS  RESTARTS AGE
my-app-5d4f7     1/1   Running 0        2d
commands.commonMistake: Not specifying namespace with -n defaults to default—miss issues in production namespace

commands.whatItDoes: Streams stdout/stderr from the container inside the pod
commands.realUseCase: Debug application errors and issues. Essential for troubleshooting failed pods.
commands.example:
kubectl logs my-app-5d4f7
[INFO] Server started on port 8080
[ERROR] Database connection failed
commands.commonMistake: Pod logs are ephemeral—when pod is deleted, logs are gone. Use centralized logging!

commands.whatItDoes: Shows events, resource requests/limits, environment variables, and status
commands.realUseCase: Understand why a pod failed to start or is stuck pending. Great for debugging.
commands.example:
kubectl describe pod my-app-5d4f7
Events:
  Type     Reason              Age  Message
  Warning  FailedScheduling    1m   0/3 nodes are available
commands.commonMistake: Not checking Events section when pod is not running—the error is there!

commands.description: Open interactive shell inside a running pod
commands.whatItDoes: Executes a command (usually bash) inside the pod for debugging
commands.realUseCase: Debug application state, inspect filesystem, check running processes
commands.commonMistake: Using exec too much for debugging instead of improving observability

commands.whatItDoes: Creates or removes pod replicas to match the desired count
commands.realUseCase: Increase replicas during traffic spikes, decrease during low traffic to save costs
commands.example:
kubectl scale deployment my-app --replicas=5
deployment.apps/my-app scaled
commands.commonMistake: Manual scaling is temporary—set HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) for automatic scaling

commands.description: Check rollout progress of a deployment
commands.whatItDoes: Shows if deployment is successfully rolling out new version or stuck
commands.realUseCase: Verify deployment is progressing before declaring success in CI/CD
commands.example:
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
deployment 'my-app' successfully rolled out
commands.commonMistake: Not waiting for rollout to complete before assuming deployment succeeded

commands.whatItDoes: Reverts deployment to the previously running pod spec
commands.realUseCase: Emergency rollback if new version has critical bugs or issues
commands.example:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app
deployment.apps/my-app rolled back
commands.commonMistake: Rolling back without investigating the root cause of the failure

comparisons.title: Kubernetes vs Docker Swarm
comparisons.whenToUse: Use Kubernetes for production at any scale. Use Swarm for small hobby projects or learning.

beginnerMistakes.title: Not Setting Resource Requests/Limits
beginnerMistakes.description: Pods with no resource limits can consume all cluster resources, causing other pods to crash
beginnerMistakes.howToFix: Always set requests (minimum guaranteed) and limits (maximum allowed) in pod spec. Example: requests: {cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi}

beginnerMistakes.title: Using 'latest' Tag for Images
beginnerMistakes.description: Never know what version is actually running. Deployments might roll back to old image unexpectedly.
beginnerMistakes.howToFix: Always use immutable tags like v1.2.3 or git commit SHA. Never use 'latest' in production.

realWorldUsage.name: Google
realWorldUsage.usage: Google created Kubernetes. They run billions of containers using K8s and offer GKE.

realWorldUsage.name: Netflix
realWorldUsage.usage: Netflix runs millions of containers across multiple regions using Kubernetes for automatic failover and scaling.

realWorldUsage.name: Airbnb
realWorldUsage.usage: Airbnb uses Kubernetes for massive-scale deployments with hundreds of microservices.

productionSection.scaling: Use HPA to auto-scale based on CPU/memory. VPA for right-sizing containers. Cluster Autoscaler to add/remove nodes.
productionSection.security: Use Network Policies to restrict pod traffic. RBAC for fine-grained access control. Pod Security Standards.
productionSection.monitoring: Use Prometheus to scrape metrics. Grafana for visualization. AlertManager for alerts.
productionSection.cicd: Integrate kubectl with CI/CD pipelines. Use GitOps (ArgoCD) for declarative deployments.

labs.title: Deploy Your First App
labs.expectedOutput: All nginx pods should show Running status

interviewQuestions.question: What is a pod and how does it relate to Docker containers?
interviewQuestions.answer: A pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, similar to a container in Docker. However, a pod can contain one or more containers that share networking (same IP) and storage.
interviewQuestions.example: Most pods have 1 container. Some have helper containers (sidecars) for logging or monitoring.
interviewQuestions.followUp: Why would you want multiple containers in a single pod?

pod_commands.description: List all pods in current namespace
List all pods in all namespaces
